behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode spans two module boundaries — the HMAC verification module must reject the request AND the handler must honour that rejection by withholding the queue write. A pure unit test of the HMAC function alone would pass even if the handler never calls it or ignores its result. REAL-INFRA: No real HTTP server, OS, or subprocess is needed; an in-process fake request object with a spy on the queue write method is sufficient to catch the actual failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the HMAC function itself would hide whether the handler is wired to call it at all. REJECTION-GATE for System: The failure does not depend on real TCP, real network, or real OS behaviour; an in-process fake HTTP context reproduces it completely.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the webhook handler with a spy-wrapped fake queue module. Send a POST with a body and an X-Signature-256 header computed from a different body. Assert the response status is 401 and that the queue's write method was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The ordering constraint — queue write completes before the response is flushed — only becomes observable when the handler and the queue module interact. A unit test of either component in isolation cannot verify the sequence across the boundary. REAL-INFRA: The ordering can be captured with an instrumented in-process queue stub that records its write timestamp alongside the timestamp at which the HTTP response callback is invoked; no real external queue, OS subprocess, or network socket is required to reproduce the failure. MOCK-HIDE: A fully mocked queue that returns immediately regardless of order would trivially satisfy the timing assertion. REJECTION-GATE for System: The sequence of 'queue.write() resolved → response header sent' is fully reproducible inside a Node.js in-process HTTP context (e.g. supertest/inject).
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler in-process with an instrumented queue stub that records a high-resolution timestamp on each write. Send a valid signed webhook. Capture the timestamp at which the 200 response is received. Assert the queue write timestamp is strictly earlier than the response receipt timestamp.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The fire-and-forget pattern spans the boundary between the handler and the downstream processor. If the handler awaits the processor, the test fails; if it doesn't, the test passes. This cannot be verified by testing either component alone. REAL-INFRA: The failure (handler blocking on downstream) manifests in Node.js's event loop and is fully reproducible with an in-process fake downstream processor that delays 10 seconds via an unresolved Promise. No real subprocess, network, or OS primitive is needed. REJECTION-GATE for System: A slow downstream can be injected as a simple in-process stub that never resolves within the test window; real HTTP connections add no diagnostic value for this specific behaviour.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a fake downstream processor whose work function returns a Promise that does not resolve for 10 seconds. Send a valid signed webhook in-process. Assert that the HTTP 200 response is received in well under 10 seconds (e.g. < 500 ms), proving the handler did not await the processor.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a concurrent-load timing requirement. The failure — event-loop starvation or improper serialization — only surfaces when multiple real simultaneous connections compete for the server's event loop. REAL-INFRA: In-process injection tools process requests sequentially on the same event loop tick, masking blocking behaviour that only appears under genuine concurrent TCP connections. Real concurrent HTTP clients hitting a real bound socket are required to reproduce the failure honestly. Wall-clock constraints under real concurrency are the canonical System-level indicator. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: A fake in-process HTTP context cannot produce the backpressure from independent OS TCP connections that triggers the failure. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic/Workflow: No LLM or multi-step agent logic is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service as a real HTTP server on a local port. Configure downstream processing to sleep 15 seconds per event. Fire 10 concurrent real HTTP requests using parallel HTTP client calls. Assert that every response arrives within 3 seconds of the request being sent, measured with wall-clock timestamps on the client side.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Deduplication requires the ID store and the handler to interact: the handler must query the store before deciding whether to enqueue, and then record the ID. Testing either in isolation cannot verify the round-trip. REAL-INFRA: A real in-process ID store (e.g. an in-memory Set, SQLite table, or the actual module used for persistence) is sufficient; no external network or OS subprocess is needed to reproduce the duplicate-write failure. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the ID store to always return 'not seen' would never exercise the deduplication path, hiding a missing or incorrectly wired check. REJECTION-GATE for System: The ID lookup and write are local module operations; real HTTP adds no diagnostic value for this specific failure.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler in-process with its real ID store and a spy-wrapped queue. Send two valid signed POST requests carrying identical event IDs. Assert the queue write spy was called exactly once across both requests, and that the second request returns the same 200 acknowledgment.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The handler must correctly interpret the queue's 'full' signal and translate it into both the status code and the Retry-After header. Neither the queue nor the handler alone exposes this mapping; the failure is at their interface. REAL-INFRA: A fake queue stub configured to throw a 'queue full' error is sufficient — no real broker, OS resource, or network is needed to reproduce the failure. MOCK-HIDE: A queue mock that always succeeds would never exercise the error-handling path. REJECTION-GATE for System: The queue's full signal is an in-process error; real infrastructure is not required to produce it.
behaviors.test_description: Start the handler with a fake queue whose write method always throws a 'QUEUE_FULL' error. Send a valid signed webhook. Assert the HTTP response status is 503 and the response includes a Retry-After header with a non-empty value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior is about the queue module itself, not the handler. The failure is internal to the queue: its write path must check the current depth against the configured limit before accepting a new entry. Testing via a mock would test the mock, not the real enforcement logic. REAL-INFRA: The queue's backing store (whether SQLite, an append-only file, or an in-process data structure) must actually persist count state across writes — an in-process real instantiation of the queue module with its real storage adapter is sufficient. No external network, OS subprocess, or real broker is required. REJECTION-GATE for System: The capacity check is a local read-compare-write within the queue module's own storage; it does not require OS-level resources or a real external service to manifest.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the persistent queue module directly with capacity=3 using its real implementation (not a mock). Enqueue 3 events and assert all succeed. Attempt to enqueue a 4th event and assert that the queue throws or returns a 'full' rejection. Verify queue depth remains at 3.
